  "bio": "Mariano Duncan February 23, 2023 / in BioProject - Person 1990s All-Stars Dominican Republic / by admin LnRiLWhlYWRpbmcuaGFzLWJhY2tncm91bmR7cGFkZGluZzowfQ==   This article was written by Malcolm Allen When the first big-leaguers from the Dominican Republic became All-Stars and competed in the World Series in 1962, Mariano Duncan was in his mother\u2019s womb. He grew up to start one All-Star Game and play for two World Series champions \u2013 the 1990 Cincinnati Reds and 1996 New York Yankees. Primarily a middle infielder, Duncan spent 12 seasons (1985-1987, 1989-1997) in the majors with five teams. He was inducted into the Dominican Sports Hall of Fame in 2008. 1 Mariano Duncan Nolasco was born on March 13, 1963, in San Pedro de Macor\u00eds. His parents \u2013 Enrique Duncan and Nilda Nolasco \u2013 had 11 children: eight boys and three girls. 2 They shared a tin shack in Angelina, a mill town surrounding the city\u2019s oldest sugar refinery. One of their neighbors was future big-leaguer Rafael Ram\u00edrez . 3 Duncan\u2019s grandfather was part of the wave of migrants from mostly British-controlled islands who had come to the Dominican Republic to cut sugar cane; hence his English surname. 4 After losing a leg working in the cane fields, Mariano\u2019s father became a shoemaker. Nilda was a fruit, vegetables, and poultry vendor. \u201cSometimes Mariano helped me sell, but most of the time, it was pelota, pelota, pelota (ball, ball, ball),\u201d she recalled. \u201cHe would say, \u2018Don\u2019t worry, Mother, when I become a ballplayer, we will be living well.\u2019\u201d 5 Three of Mariano\u2019s younger brothers also played professionally. Twins Andr\u00e9s and Enrique (b. 1971) peaked in Triple A and Single A, respectively, while Carlos (b. 1977) reached Double A. Duncan and his peers, including Tony Fern\u00e1ndez and Juan Samuel , initially used cardboard gloves, rolled-up sock balls, and tree branches for bats. 6 He was a fan of the Houston Astros and played center field like their Dominican star, C\u00e9sar Cede\u00f1o , who was also a winter-ball standout for the San Pedro de Macor\u00eds-based Estrellas Orientales. 7 \u201cI used to cry when my mother wouldn\u2019t give me money to go see him play,\u201d he said. 8 Duncan received his first batting glove from another Estrellas player, Cincinnati Reds third baseman Ray Knight . 9 \u201cI used to go to sleep every single night with that batting glove,\u201d he recalled. 10 At age 15, Duncan stopped attending Liceo Gast\u00f3n Fernando Deligne to concentrate on baseball. Two years later, the Los Angeles Dodgers rejected him at a tryout. \u201cBut after that, I seemed to get stronger every day,\u201d he said. 11 Duncan advanced to one of the country\u2019s top amateur teams, Bater\u00edas Meteoro, in Santo Domingo. 12 On January 17, 1982, Dodgers\u2019 scouts Ralph \u00c1vila and Elvio Jim\u00e9nez signed him for $5,000. 13 Intrigued by Duncan\u2019s speed, the Dodgers made him a shortstop and switch-hitter. In 30 appearances with their rookie-level Pioneer League affiliate in Lethbridge (Alberta, Canada) that summer, he committed 15 errors and struck out in 21 of his 55 at-bats. \u201cI almost quit and [went] home. I feel so depressed, so far away from my family,\u201d he said. 14 Yet he persevered. In 1983, Duncan batted .266 and led the Class A Florida State League with 56 steals and 15 triples in 109 games, mostly as an outfielder. His Vero Beach Dodgers won the championship, and he swiped four bases in the title game. 15 Next, Duncan played winter ball in Colombia. His manager with the Indios de Cartagena was Mike Brito, the scout who stood out during television broadcasts from Dodger Stadium; standing behind the backstop in his white Panama hat, with his white Stalker radar gun trained on the pitcher. 16 \u201cThe press and the owners wanted me to get rid of [Duncan] because he struck out so many times,\u201d Brito recalled. \u201cI told them, \u2018You have to be patient with this kid\u2019\u2026 Mariano started making contact, and he stole a lot of bases, and he was like an idol.\u201d 17 In 1984, Duncan manned second base for the San Antonio Dodgers in the Double-A Texas League. He tied for tops in the circuit with 11 triples and batted .253. Next, he joined the Dominican Winter League\u2019s Tigres de Licey. He didn\u2019t play much (.149 in 47 at-bats) for the Santo Domingo-based team, but they won the title, plus the Caribbean Series in Mazatl\u00e1n, Mexico. 18 Duncan reported to Albuquerque, New Mexico, home of the Dodgers\u2019 Triple-A Pacific Coast League affiliate, after 1985 spring training. But less than 24 hours before L.A.\u2019s season opener, he was told to report to the Astrodome . 19 \u201cI thought it was one of my friends calling me up, joking with me. I couldn\u2019t believe I was going to the major leagues,\u201d he said. 20 Steve Sax had injured his ankle in the final exhibition game, so Duncan replaced him as the Dodgers\u2019 second baseman and leadoff hitter. Facing future Hall of Famer Nolan Ryan , Duncan struck out on three pitches in his first at-bat. 21 He finished 0-for-4 with an error that allowed the decisive run to score. Duncan\u2019s first two hits were singles against knuckleballer Joe Niekro the following night. In Niekro\u2019s next start, at Dodger Stadium, Duncan completed his first week in the majors with a tie-breaking seventh-inning homer, batting left-handed. That weekend in San Diego, his first righty round-tripper secured another victory. Two days later, he pulled his left hamstring and missed nine games. By the time he returned to action, Sax was back at second base, but starting shortstop Dave Anderson was on the disabled list. Although Duncan had not played there regularly since rookie ball, Dodgers manager Tom Lasorda installed him at shortstop on May 5. \u201cThe first three weeks, I made a lot of errors, but [coach] Monty Basgall worked very hard with me,\u201d Duncan said. 22 That summer, Basgall compared the 22-year-old Duncan to a perennial Gold Glover. \u201cMariano has a better arm than Ozzie Smith and he can outrun Ozzie. He\u2019s not in Ozzie\u2019s class yet, but he\u2019s got a chance to be there.\u201d 23 The following spring, Lasorda opined that Duncan was more advanced than Smith as a rookie and predicted that he would eventually outhit him. 24 Initially, Duncan lived with Dodgers slugger \u2013 and fellow San Pedro de Macor\u00eds native \u2013 Pedro Guerrero . \u201cGuerrero is like my mother, my father and my brother,\u201d he said. 25 On August 23, 1985, they hit grand slams in consecutive innings to lead L.A.\u2019s comeback victory in Montreal. 26 With Duncan serving as the primary leadoff hitter down the stretch, the Dodgers won the NL West. In 142 games, he batted .244 with six homers and 38 steals. At least three of his 24 doubles came on bunts. 27 That fall, he finished third in NL Rookie of the Year Award voting. The Dodgers lost the NLCS in six games to the Cardinals, who had picked up C\u00e9sar Cede\u00f1o for the September stretch drive. Duncan hit .222 (4-for-18) with two doubles and a triple, and missed one contest after he was spiked below his left knee. Duncan moved his family into a new home \u2013 big-league pitcher Joaqu\u00edn And\u00fajar \u2019s former residence in San Pedro de Macor\u00eds. In 37 appearances for Licey that winter, Duncan batted .303 with a Dominican League-leading eight triples. One week before Opening Day 1986, Guerrero suffered a serious knee injury. Desperate for offense, L.A. signed free agent C\u00e9sar Cede\u00f1o. \u201cWhen I saw myself on the same field, wearing the same uniform as that guy,\u201d Duncan said. \u201cIt was my dream come true.\u201d 28 But Duncan didn\u2019t raise his batting average above .200 to stay until June 4, and the 35-year-old Cede\u00f1o \u2013 also struggling \u2013 was released shortly thereafter. The Dodgers entered the All-Star break in last place. \u201cA lot of the guys were hurt but I was one Tommy [Lasorda] came down on,\u201d Duncan said. \u201cLasorda had lost faith in me and I started to lose faith in myself\u2026 There\u2019s a lot of pressures when you\u2019re that young and in a new country and playing for a team in a big city with all of the expectations by the media and fans.\u201d 29 Duncan\u2019s sophomore jinx continued. On August 18, he broke his left foot when he was hit by a thrown ball. 30 He missed more than a month and finished the year batting .229 in 109 games. His inside-the-park homer on June 9 and career-high 48 stolen bases were highlights. Before undergoing surgery to remove fluid and reduce swelling in his left knee that fall, Duncan worked on cutting down his swing in the Arizona Instructional League. 31 \u201cWe\u2019re very happy with the fact that Mariano volunteered,\u201d reported Dodgers Vice President Al Campanis . \u201cWe don\u2019t want him to be a power hitter. We want him to get on base and steal bases.\u201d 32 Duncan had batted .273 right-handed, but just .217 as a lefty in his first two seasons. Six games into the 1987 campaign, he became a fulltime righty swinger. \u201cIt was a decision I\u2019ve wanted to [make] for a long time,\u201d he explained. \u201cCampanis wanted me to hit lefthanded.\u201d 33 The change became possible after Campanis was fired for making racially offensive remarks on television\u2019s Nightline . That June, Duncan left the field on a stretcher twice in an 11-day span. First, he was upended on a takeout slide in Atlanta and bruised his left hip. 34 After he was spiked in Houston, his right knee required stitches. 35 Duncan returned after a stint on the 15-day disabled list, but he committed three errors in the second inning at Dodger Stadium on July 23. Before exiting the contest with a migraine after five frames, he tipped his cap to heckling fans. 36 The Dodgers demoted him to Albuquerque. \u201cIt wasn\u2019t just one game. If we had to send him down for one bad game, we\u2019d have done it a long time ago,\u201d Lasorda said. 37 When Duncan was recalled 10 days later, Lasorda observed, \u201cHe\u2019s not going to be a good ballplayer unless he hits the ball on a line and bunts for hits.\u201d 38 On August 16, Duncan slid into first base attempting to beat out a bunt single and tore a ligament in his left knee, ending his season. 39 In 76 games, he batted .215. That fall, the Dodgers signed free agent shortstop Alfredo Griffin . While Duncan was hitting .244 for Licey and leading the Dominican League in triples. Griffin met with him in Santo Domingo. \u201cHe [told] me to ask him if I have any questions. I try to stay as close to him as I can,\u201d Duncan said. Griffin encouraged him to swing down on the ball, hit to the opposite field, and not to try to do too much. \u201cAlfredo does all those things better than him,\u201d observed Dodgers coach Manny Mota , a Dominican like the two infielders. \u201c[Duncan] gets frustrated too much. He needs to concentrate more.\u201d 40 Duncan\u2019s 1988 season was a disaster. Early in spring training, he had a minor clubhouse altercation with L.A.s biggest offseason acquisition, outfielder Kirk Gibson . 41 On March 22, Duncan and Guerrero missed the team bus and the beginning of an exhibition game. 42 Five days later, Duncan was optioned to Triple-A Albuquerque. He responded by calling Lasorda a liar, insisting that he had been promised a place on L.A.\u2019s Opening Day roster, a contention that the manager disputed. 43 Duncan threatened to quit baseball, but his father, agent, Guerrero, and Griffin talked him out of it. 44 He had played second base in winter ball at the Dodgers\u2019 request. \u201cNow I\u2019m playing shortstop in the minor leagues. It just doesn\u2019t make sense to me,\u201d Duncan said. 45 When Griffin broke his hand on May 21, Duncan was sidelined by a strained hamstring, so, L.A. called up Mike Sharperson instead. 46 In June, Duncan missed several weeks with a stress fracture in one of his ribs. 47 On July 25, he broke the hamate bone in his left wrist swinging at a pitch, ending his season after appearing in just 56 minor league games. 48 Meanwhile, the Dodgers won the World Series. That winter, Duncan stole 17 bases in 48 games for Licey and earned a Dominican League Gold Glove for his play at second base. During spring training 1989, he said, \u201cI have a good attitude now\u2026 I have learned from my mistakes, I am more mature.\u201d Some speculated that he would benefit from Guerrero\u2019s departure \u2013 the slugger had been traded to the Cardinals the previous summer. \u201cPeople think Pete [Guerrero] was a bad influence on me,\u201d Duncan acknowledged. \u201cBut Pete tried to help me. He really did. He said I got mad too fast. He never tried to teach me bad things.\u201d 49 Duncan made L.A.\u2019s Opening Day roster and appeared in 49 of the first 92 games, though only 16 were starts. On July 18, he was traded to the Cincinnati Reds with pitcher Tim Leary for outfielder Kal Daniels and utilityman Lenny Harris . The Reds\u2019 Barry Larkin had injured his throwing arm during the All-Star Game, so Duncan started most of Cincinnati\u2019s remaining games at shortstop. \u201cOne day I\u2019m in the Dodger doghouse, the next day I\u2019m in the Reds lineup. I was the happiest man in the world,\u201d he said. 50 On August 4, Duncan hit his only career walk-off homer, against the Braves\u2019 Joe Boever . When he took Atlanta\u2019s Derek Lilliquist deep the following evening, it was the only time Duncan ever led off a game with a round-tripper. Reds skipper Pete Rose was banned from baseball for gambling a few weeks later, and Cincinnati hired Lou Piniella as their manager for 1990. Duncan, formerly a 6-foot, 160-pound rookie, weighed 192 for his first spring training with his new team after adding 12 pounds of upper body muscle during the offseason. As the Reds\u2019 Opening Day second baseman, he homered against Mike Scott at the Astrodome. Through May 10, Duncan\u2019s .400 batting average led the majors, and he credited tips from Piniella and hitting coach Tony P\u00e9rez . \u201cMy dream is to hit .300,\u201d he said. \u201cFrom what I see, everything Tony and Lou says works. I seem to improve with everything they teach me.\u201d 51 In 125 games, Duncan batted .306 with an NL-high 11 triples. His Adjusted OPS+ of 120 was also a career high. The Reds won the NL West and defeated the Pittsburgh Pirates in a six-game NLCS. In Game Three at Three Rivers Stadium , Duncan went 3-for-5 with four RBIs, including a tie-breaking three-run homer off Zane Smith . Cincinnati won the World Series by sweeping the Oakland Athletics. Although Duncan was in the lineup for every postseason game, the Reds\u2019 original plan was for Bill Doran to start against righties. But the switch-hitter \u2013 acquired from Houston on August 30 \u2013 was hospitalized with...",

{
  "name": "Ken Griffey Sr.",
  "bio": "Ken Griffey Jr. January 13, 2016 / in BioProject - Person 500 Home Runs , Home Run Champions Hall of Fame , Most Valuable Player 1990s All-Stars , 2000s All-Stars Parent-Child Nuclear Powered Baseball / by admin LnRiLWhlYWRpbmcuaGFzLWJhY2tncm91bmR7cGFkZGluZzowfQ==   Emily Hawks While the honor of having the sweetest swing in baseball may seem like it\u2019s a subjective one, few would disagree that Ken Griffey Jr. possessed the sweetest swing there ever was. He was a natural, and his inborn abilities coupled with his youthful enthusiasm ignited an entire city\u2019s passion for baseball. Behind the center-field wall at Seattle\u2019s Safeco Field, beneath the feet of the fans donning backwards baseball caps as a tribute to \u201cThe Kid,\u201d one can find two special bricks installed when the stadium opened in 1999. The brick on the left reads \u201cTrey + Taryn Griffey,\u201d and the brick on the right, \u201cThe House Their Father Built.\u201d It wouldn\u2019t be long after the mortar had dried that Griffey would leave for what he had hoped would be a storybook return to his hometown of Cincinnati. But frequent trips to the disabled list stifled the talents of one of the greatest center fielders to play the game, and Griffey\u2019s carefree attitude seemed to dissipate along with his playing time. Still, although it is tempting to contemplate what could have been were it not for those injuries, it is still gratifying to marvel at what was. Griffey\u2019s effortless home-run swings, dazzling center-field catches, and 1,000-watt smile lit up the game for an entire generation of baseball fans, and few would dispute that he saved baseball in Seattle. George Kenneth Griffey Jr. was born on November 21, 1969, in Donora, Pennsylvania to parents George Kenneth Sr. and Alberta, better known as Birdie. He shared a birthday and a birthplace with Stan Musial , who was born exactly 49 years earlier in Donora, a former steel town 20 miles south of Pittsburgh on the Monongahela River. Griffey was followed 18 months later by a younger brother, Craig, as well as a sister, Lathesia, two years after Craig. With Griffey Sr., a baseball star himself, the younger Ken Griffey grew up in major-league clubhouses, often spotted in a miniature Reds uniform at Riverfront Stadium surrounded by stars like Pete Rose and Johnny Bench . For the Griffeys, it was about family time. \u201cMy dad didn\u2019t care if we watched him play baseball,\u201d said Junior. \u201cHe cared about spending quality time with us.\u201d 1 Junior\u2019s time in the clubhouse with his dad would also influence his own eventual path in the major leagues. When Griffey Sr. was with the Yankees, manager Billy Martin requested through one of his coaches that Griffey\u2019s kids be removed from the clubhouse after they\u2019d been playing in the stadium corridors with the other players\u2019 kids before and after a blowout loss. Though 14 kids had joined them, Martin allegedly only wanted the Griffeys gone. 2 From that moment, Griffey Jr. had distaste for the Yankees and ultimately vowed never to play for them. \u201cI don\u2019t forget things like that,\u201d he said. \u201cAnd I never will.\u201d 3 It wasn\u2019t long before the standout high-school athlete piqued the interest of major-league scouts. One such scout was Tom Mooney. Mariners\u2019 owner George Argyros had directed general manager Hal Keller to promote his then-chauffeur Mooney to the scouting staff in 1984, and Mooney went on to cover Ohio, Michigan, and Indiana for the Mariners. After an abysmal last-place finish in 1986, the Mariners had the first pick in the 1987 draft, and their selection was still very much in the air. In the spring of 1987 Mariners director of scouting Roger Jongewaard joined Mooney to watch Griffey\u2019s Moeller High School team play in a new community park in Cincinnati. The park had a grove of trees roughly 20 yards beyond the outfield fence. In his second at-bat, Griffey lobbed one high into right field and well over the fence. \u201cWhich tree did it hit?\u201d Jongewaard asked. \u201cRoger,\u201d said Mooney, \u201cit went over the trees.\u201d 4 High-profile baseball names were also taking note. \u201cI saw Ken Griffey Jr. in high school at Moeller High in Cincinnati and he was the best prospect I\u2019ve ever seen in my life,\u201d said Bobby Cox , then general manager of the Atlanta Braves. `\u201dThere was nobody even close to him; he was outstanding.\u201d 5 Still, Mariners ownership was hesitant to take on another high schooler after their 1986 first-round pick, Pat Lennon , had flopped. Also, Griffey had failed a psychological test that baseball teams used to forecast a player\u2019s behavior and character. In fact, his test result was the worst the Mariners had ever seen. 6 While Griffey was averse to retaking the test, he desperately wanted to be the number one pick in the draft. \u201cThe thing in our favor was that he knew everyone would remember who the No. 1 pick was,\u201d Mooney recalled. \u201cHe was very proud of that and wanted to show his dad.\u201d 7 So Mooney re-administered the test one afternoon at the Griffey house. This time Griffey had an average result. More importantly, though, Mooney saw firsthand in Griffey\u2019s home how he interacted with his mother, grandmother, brother, and friends. He declared him a normal kid, and the endorsement trickled up to the front office. Argyros remained skeptical, but he reluctantly agreed to selecting Griffey after Griffey and his agent agreed to sign for just $160,000. When agent Brian Goldberg told Griffey he might be leaving tens of thousands of dollars on the table, Griffey replied, \u201cI\u2019ll make up for that later. Let\u2019s be No. 1.\u201d 8 In spite of a seemingly bright future, Griffey struggled with turmoil in his teenage years. \u201cIt seemed like everyone was yelling at me in baseball, then I came home and everyone was yelling at me there,\u201d said Griffey. \u201cI got depressed. I got angry. I didn\u2019t want to live.\u201d 9 In the summer of 1987, Griffey was playing for the Bellingham Mariners of the short-season Class-A Northwest League. While on the team bus one day, Griffey got into a dispute with the teenage sons of the bus driver. One of them had reportedly expressed racial epithets toward Griffey, while another allegedly threatened him with a gun. Griffey recalled, \u201cGrowing up back home I never had to deal with anything like that.\u201d 10 When he returned home to Ohio that fall, he was regularly staying out until the early-morning hours, often worrying his mother. Griffey Sr. decided that his son should either pay rent or get his own place. Griffey Jr. recalled, \u201cI was confused. I was hurting and I wanted to cause some hurt for others.\u201d He had contemplated suicide many times, and on one fateful day in January 1988 he followed his thoughts with action. A 17-year-old Griffey ingested 277 aspirin and wound up in an intensive-care unit in Mount Airy, Ohio. Shortly thereafter, a frightened and angry Griffey Sr. arrived at the hospital and a fight commenced. Though Birdie was distressed, she knew the primary source of conflict and angst for Junior originated with Senior and opted to stay out of the fray. Eventually Griffey Jr. moved into his own condo. While the arguments continued between father and son, an increased understanding began to develop between the two of them. Griffey quickly ascended the minor leagues. His first hit with the Bellingham team in 1987 was a home run, and he finished his first professional season in Low A, batting .313 in 54 games. In 1988, after he clubbed 74 hits in just 58 games for San Bernardino of the Class-A California League, the team retired his jersey. He was promoted to Vermont of the Double-A Eastern League later that season, and the mere 17 games there would be his last as a full-time minor leaguer. Griffey joined the big-league club in Tempe, Arizona, for spring training in 1989, and it didn\u2019t take long for him to make a splash in the Cactus League. He hit .359 that spring and compiled a 15-game hitting streak. He also set Mariners preseason records in three categories with 32 hits, 49 total bases, and 20 RBIs. 11 He had extra incentive to make the Opening Day squad, as his father told him, \u201cThis could be my last season, so you had better make the team.\u201d 12 The baseball world quickly took note. Prior to the 1989 season, Baseball America ranked Griffey the best number one draft pick since the draft\u2019s inception in 1966. While an Opening Day roster spot seemed like a foregone conclusion, manager Jim Lefebvre couldn\u2019t resist giving Griffey a hard time. \u201cAt the end of spring training, he called me into his office,\u201d Griffey recalled. \u201cHe goes through, like, five minutes of stuff like \u2018These decisions are so tough,\u2019 \u2018You know, we\u2019ve got a lot of veterans that have earned their chance,\u2019 then at the end, he sticks out his hand from across his desk and says, \u2018You\u2019re my starting center fielder.\u2019 \u2026 And he had managed to keep a straight face the whole time.\u201d 13 Like Lefebvre, Griffey\u2019s teammates also couldn\u2019t help but give the rookie a razzing about his big promotion. The Mariners had one last exhibition game in Las Vegas prior to the season opener in Oakland, and it happened to fall on April Fools\u2019 Day. \u201cI walk into the clubhouse, and everyone\u2019s talking about a big trade we just made for Dale Murphy . Which would mean he\u2019s our new center fielder, not me,\u201d remembered Griffey. \u201cSo, I make that long walk to Lefebvre\u2019s office, and he says, \u2018I guess you\u2019ve heard about the big trade we made?\u2019 And again, he goes on and on, then finally he says, \u2018Do you know today\u2019s date?\u2019\u201d 14 Griffey made his major-league debut on April 3, 1989, at the Oakland Coliseum , facing Athletics ace Dave Stewart . \u201cWe got to Oakland, and man, I\u2019m nervous,\u201d Griffey recalled. \u201cDave Stewart\u2019s on the mound, and he could have rolled the ball up there, I would have swung.\u201d 15 In spite of his nerves, Griffey hammered a 375-foot double to left-center in his first at-bat on the second pitch he saw. \u201cHe was scary all night long,\u201d remarked Oakland manager Tony La Russa . \u201cThe young man has a world of talent. \u2026 He\u2019s going to be something to contend with.\u201d 16 One week later, in his home debut at the Kingdome, Griffey hit his first major-league home run, launching the first pitch he saw from White Sox pitcher Eric King into the left-field seats. 17 Griffey\u2019s quick success led to a near-instantaneous fan following in Seattle. Just over a month after his debut, a Seattle-area trading-card company launched a Ken Griffey Jr. candy bar. Demand quickly soared, and ultimately nearly one million bars were purchased. 18 Ironically, Griffey was allergic to chocolate. 19 By late July, Griffey was a leading candidate for the American League Rookie of the Year award, batting .287 with 13 homers and 45 RBIs. But on July 25 he suffered a fracture in his right hand, purportedly after falling while coming out of the shower in his Chicago hotel room. 20 However, club officials later admitted that the injury occurred when Griffey slammed his hand in anger against a hotel-room wall during an argument with a girlfriend. 21 The incident suggested to Mariners management that Griffey still had a lot of maturing to do. Griffey, who was raised by his mother and grandmother, often struggled with homesickness. \u201cHe was the only player I\u2019ve ever dealt with where I\u2019d have to call his mother,\u201d said Mariners GM Woody Woodward . 22 He missed nearly a month of playing time as he healed. After returning to the lineup on August 20, Griffey finished out the year with an additional three home runs and 16 RBIs and ended the season batting .264. He finished third in AL Rookie of the Year voting. He did receive one first-place vote, presumably from Bob Finnigan of the Seattle Times , who wrote, \u201cI will vote for Ken Griffey Jr. \u2026 He has brought to the Mariners an exuberance long missing. \u2026 Seeing Griffey play almost every day, there is no way I cannot vote for him.\u201d 23 As the 1989 season came to a close, Griffey\u2019s future in Seattle seemed bright. He pondered the possibility of his father joining him in Seattle. \u201cNothing would make him happier than to be in one game with me,\u201d said Griffey. 24 While Griffey had become known for his swing, he was also increasingly becoming known for his glove. On April 26, 1990, with Randy Johnson on the mound, Yankees right fielder Jesse Barfield , having homered off Johnson in his first at-bat, was aiming for a repeat, and with a loud crack of the bat he launched Johnson\u2019s offering deep to left-center. Griffey ran to the fence, gave it a quick glance, and dug a spike into the soft padding, hoisting his shoulders above the 7-foot-3 wall at Yankee Stadium . With his perfectly timed leap, he robbed Barfield of what would have been his 200th home run. Griffey\u2019s wide grin could be seen even before he landed. His father, who was seated next to Barfield\u2019s wife in the stands, commented, \u201cI think (the smile) was for me.\u201d 25 Though the Griffeys had already made history in 1989, becoming the first father-son combination to play in the major leagues in the same season, that storyline was eclipsed in 1990. Griffey Sr. was released by the Reds on August 18 and was picked up by the Mariners on the 29th, a move the Mariners hoped would help the younger Griffey mature. \u201cA big reason we signed his father was so he would be with him,\u201d said Jongewaard. 26 On August 31 the two Griffeys became the first father and son tandem in major-league history to play in a game on the same team, with Griffey Sr. in left and Griffey Jr. in center. That evening the two hit back-to-back singles in the bottom of the first. \u201cI wanted to cry or something,\u201d said Junior after the game. \u201cIt just seemed like a father-son game, like we were out playing catch in the backyard. But we were actually playing a real game.\u201d 27 The fairytale culminated two weeks later on September 14 in a game against the California Angels in Anaheim. In the first inning, Griffey Sr., facing Angels starter Kirk McCaskill , hammered an 0-and-2 pitch 402 feet over the center-field wall. He was greeted at home plate by his son. \u201cI felt for him then,\u201d said Senior. \u201cI knew he would be thinking home run. I could see it in his eyes when I crossed the plate.\u201d 28 What Senior saw in Junior\u2019s eyes soon became reality. Junior came to the plate and ran the count to 3-and-0.   "name": "Roger Clemens",
  "bio": "Roger Clemens January 4, 2016 / in BioProject - Person 300 Pitching Victories Cy Young Award , Most Valuable Player 1980s All-Stars , 1990s All-Stars , 2000s All-Stars 2026 Hall of Fame ballot 1986 Boston Red Sox , Nuclear Powered Baseball / by admin LnRiLWhlYWRpbmcuaGFzLWJhY2tncm91bmR7cGFkZGluZzowfQ==   This article was written by Frederick C. Bush Roger Clemens\u2019 last major-league start, on October 7, 2007 \u2014 for the New York Yankees against the Cleveland Indians, the very team against which he had made his major-league debut in May 1984 \u2014 ended with him limping off the mound after only 2\u2153 innings with a hamstring injury. Clemens had already allowed the Indians one run in each of the first and second innings, and, after facing two batters in the top of the third, he could pitch no more. He was charged with a third run, though the Yankees came back to win the game 8-4 for their lone victory in this American League Division Series. Such an ending is not what a movie screenwriter would have scripted as the final chapter of \u201cRocket\u2019s\u201d 24-year career, but at least one element of Clemens\u2019 last appearance was storybook in character: He struck out the final batter he faced, Indians catcher Victor Martinez . In spite of the abrupt end to Clemens\u2019 evening and career, as he left the mound, it seemed a certainty that he would be inducted into the National Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, New York, as soon as he passed the five-year waiting period for eligibility. Few pitchers in the history of baseball could boast anything near to his accomplishments: a record seven Cy Young Awards, 354 victories, 4,672 strikeouts, seven-time ERA leader with a career 3.12 ERA, six-time 20-game winner, five-time strikeout leader, 46 shutouts in the era of relief specialists and closers, and two-time World Series champion. He was too much of a polarizing figure in his career to exceed Tom Seaver \u2019s record of being named on 98.8 percent of the Hall of Fame ballots, but he seemed certain to be a first-ballot selectee. On December 13, 2007, little more than two months after Clemens\u2019 final Yankees start, doubt was cast over his future enshrinement among baseball\u2019s immortals when he was mentioned repeatedly in the Mitchell Report on the use of performance-enhancing drugs in baseball. In the years following the report, Clemens spent almost as much time in courtrooms as he spent on pitcher\u2019s mounds during his career. By the time his first year of eligibility for the Hall of Fame arrived in January 2013, he was named on only 37.6 percent of the ballots and, in his second year, that number declined to 35.4 percent while two of his contemporaries and fellow members of the 300-win club, Greg Maddux and Tom Glavine , were elected. Clemens\u2019 life is the tale of a fanatically driven man who worked hard to achieve his dream of stardom and attained the pinnacle of success. Jorge Posada , Clemens\u2019 catcher with the Yankees, was complimentary when he said, \u201cThe only thing he wants to do is just win.\u201d 1 Cito Gaston , Clemens\u2019 manager with the Toronto Blue Jays until he was fired toward the end of the 1997 season, intended no such praise when he commented, \u201cIt\u2019s all about him, nobody else but him.\u201d 2 Clemens\u2019 ambition gained him both fans and detractors, helped him to achieve massive success, and ultimately contributed to his fall from grace. William Roger Clemens was born on August 4, 1962, in Dayton, Ohio, the fifth child of Bill and Bess Clemens. He was only 5 months old when his mother took her children and left his father, with whom he claims to have spoken only once in his life, when he was 10 years old. Less than two years later, Bess married Woody Booher, whom Roger looked up to as a real father. But he became fatherless again at the age of 8 when Booher died of a heart attack. While his mother provided Roger with an example of the work ethic he would adopt by laboring at several jobs to support her children, he came under the tutelage of his older brother Randy, whom he idolized. In high school Randy was a shortstop on the baseball team, the star shooting guard for the basketball team, and the king of his senior prom, leading Clemens to admit, \u201cWhile I was growing up, Randy was the star as far as I was concerned.\u201d 3 Though the two brothers have become estranged, Randy\u2019s influence was immense as he \u201cinstill[ed] in his brother a simple philosophy: Either you\u2019re a winner or you\u2019re a failure.\u201d 4 It was a mantra that caused Clemens to question at times whether he was good enough to become the star athlete that both of them wanted him to be. While Clemens\u2019 baseball career dwarfs his brother Randy\u2019s high-school athletic exploits, his initial attempts to emulate his elder sibling were less than encouraging. He played baseball, basketball, and football, but distinguished himself in none of these sports. In fact, the only notable event from his youth baseball exploits was that he split starts for his 1977 squad with Kelly Krzan, who was the first girl in Ohio to play on a boys\u2019 Little League team. By the time Clemens was 15 and a high-school sophomore, Randy had married and moved to Sugar Land, Texas, a suburb 20 miles southwest of downtown Houston. Randy had failed to achieve athletic stardom of his own largely due to the development of a substance-abuse problem, but he now wanted to guide his younger brother\u2019s athletic career. After the two brothers received their mother\u2019s permission, Ohio-born-and-raised Roger Clemens made the sojourn to Texas, the state with which he has become identified. Clemens enjoyed initial success by amassing a 12-1 record and helping Sugar Land\u2019s Dulles High School win a district title, but Randy was plotting a move to more competitive fields. After watching a tournament game between two of the Houston area\u2019s premier high-school teams, Bellaire and Spring Woods, Clemens decided that he wanted to play for the latter team. Bess Clemens had moved to Houston now as well, and she made sure that her son\u2019s wish was granted. The time spent at Spring Woods High School was a mixed blessing: Clemens played for a coach, Charlie Maiorana, whom he credits for much of his knowledge about mechanics and conditioning, but he spent his junior year seeing little action on a team with two of the state\u2019s best pitching prospects. His determination showed as he became known for his workout regimen, especially his running, and he had his turn as Spring Woods\u2019 number one starting pitcher during his senior year. Still, at that point in his life, the player who came to sit at number three on the major-league strikeout list still threw too softly to draw any notice from either professional or college scouts. As a favor to Clemens, Maiorana called a colleague, Wayne Graham, the new coach at San Jacinto Junior College, to ask if he could pull any strings to get Clemens to his desired destination, the University of Texas in Austin. Graham could not accomplish that feat, but he did offer Clemens a scholarship to San Jacinto, which is where Clemens\u2019 fortunes were reversed. The failure to achieve high-school stardom resulted in the season that launched Clemens on the path to professional greatness. The year 1981 was Wayne Graham\u2019s first season to coach at any college level, but he has become a legend by guiding San Jacinto to five national junior-college championships in six years (1985-1990) \u2013 a feat that earned him Collegiate Baseball Magazine \u2019s Junior College Coach of the Century Award \u2013 and leading Houston\u2019s Rice University to the NCAA College World Series Championship in 2003. What Graham did with Clemens \u2013 turning a soft-tossing youth into a flamethrower \u2013 was an equally impressive accomplishment. He preached to Clemens that he needed to finish hard on his pitches or he would never have a chance to realize his dream of pitching in the major leagues, a message Clemens took to heart as he finished his sole season at San Jacinto with a 9-2 record while the college won the Texas Junior College Athletic Association championship. His coach\u2019s assessment was that \u201cRoger began the year as one of the guys, and he ended it as an ace.\u201d 5 Graham anticipated that Clemens would remain at San Jacinto for a second year, an expectation that was buoyed when Clemens turned down an offer from the New York Mets, who had selected him in the 12th round of the 1981 draft. Clemens went through the motions of throwing for Mets manager Joe Torre and pitching coach/legend Bob Gibson at Houston\u2019s Astrodome , but he had other plans in mind. He had been contacted by University of Texas Longhorns coach Cliff Gustafson, who was now interested in the improved pitcher. The opportunity to play at Texas had been Clemens\u2019 dream, and he pounced on it; however, he failed to contact Graham about his decision and alienated the man who had placed him on the road to stardom. Clemens fulfilled expectations at Texas , although there were some hiccups along the way. The 1982 Longhorns began their season with a 33-game winning streak that was one win shy of tying the NCAA record. Clemens, who had begun the campaign 7-0, pitched in game number 34 but lost 4-3 to the University of Houston. It was later revealed that he had bursitis while pitching that game, and he missed the next two weeks of the season. He finished 12-2 with a 1.99 ERA, but Texas was eliminated from the College World Series by Wichita State. The Longhorns suffered under the burden of high expectations in 1983 and plodded through an up-and down season. At one point, the driven Clemens became so frustrated by his personal mound setbacks that he was ready to quit the team, an example of the toll that the insecurity caused by Randy Clemens\u2019 \u201cwinner or failure\u201d mentality took on him. While he was not yet a polished pitcher, he still demonstrated great potential. Houston Astros scout Gordon Lakey reported that Clemens\u2019 delivery was not compact enough, but he believed it could be helped and that Clemens would develop more leg drive and become a power pitcher. 6 Chicago White Sox scout Larry Monroe \u2019s report echoed that of Lakey as he wrote of Clemens: \u201cDelivery is fluid but does not use body at all. Should be easily improved and no reason why he shouldn\u2019t be in low 90\u2019s. I\u2019m surprised he doesn\u2019t have shoulder problems from standing up and just throwing. Some bend in legs and drive to plate would help velocity, life, and location.\u201d 7 Both scouts projected Clemens as a likely second-round draft pick. Owing to rare encouragement from the usually gruff Gustafson, Clemens persevered \u2013 he went 13-5 with a 3.04 ERA \u2013 and the Longhorns survived their inconsistency to make a return trip to the College World Series. Before Clemens took the mound for his start against Oklahoma State in the College World Series on June 6, the Boston Red Sox selected him as the 19th player chosen in the major-league draft, a circumstance about which he said, \u201cI was completely surprised. As far as I was concerned, Boston was a foreign country.\u201d 8 Five days after defeating Oklahoma State, Clemens capped his Texas career with a complete-game 4-3 victory over Alabama in the College World Series Championship Game to put himself and his team on top of the collegiate baseball world before he departed Austin for Boston, having now been signed by Red Sox scout Danny Doyle . Of course, Clemens did not make it to the parent club straight out of college, but he did take the fast track through the Red Sox\u2019 minor-league system where he already exhibited character traits that became hallmarks of his career. His first stop was with the Winter Haven Red Sox of the Class-A Florida State League, for whom he went 3-1 with a 1.24 ERA in four starts and where he established his reputation for pitching inside to hitters. Two days before his final Winter Haven start, Clemens had taken umbrage at the Lakeland Tigers\u2019 Ronald Davis taking out his Red Sox (and ex-University of Texas) teammate Mike Brumley at second base, a play on which Brumley was injured. Clemens pitched a 15-strikeout shutout against Lakeland in which he also retaliated for Brumley\u2019s injury by hitting Davis in the head in his first at-bat. Clemens claimed \u2013 as most pitchers do \u2013 that he had only wanted to brush Davis back and that the pitch had gotten away from him; however, he also claimed that he was prepared to fight, something for which Davis was in no condition as he collapsed and was taken to a hospital. The split opinion among baseball observers as to whether Clemens merely pitched inside or was a headhunter mirrors the split in opinion about his character in general. Few players thought poorly of Don Drysdale or Nolan Ryan for pitching close inside, but these two pitchers were held in high regard while Clemens was often considered arrogant. Clemens fanned the flames of this negative reputation by both his actions and his words, never more infamously so than after winning the 1986 American League MVP Award. When informed that no less a luminary than Hank Aaron had asserted that pitchers should not receive the MVP, he retorted, \u201cI wish he was still playing. I\u2019d probably crack his head open to show him how valuable I was.\u201d 9 After his debacle-marred gem, Clemens was promoted to the New Britain (Connecticut) Red Sox of the Double-A Eastern League and amassed a 4-1 record with a 1.38 ERA in seven starts, but he also continued to draw controversy. In the team\u2019s first-round playoff series, Reading Phillies manager Bill Dancy protested that Clemens was using a glove that had writing all over it and claimed that it was distracting. The home-plate umpire ordered Clemens to use a different glove \u2013 an order the pitcher complied with \u2013 but he began to curse at Clemens due to the grief he was getting from New Britain\u2019s bench. Clemens charged the umpire but stopped short of any physical contact. Instead, he calmed down, borrowed a teammate\u2019s glove, and proceeded to dominate Reading. Charging umpires became another Clemens trait as his career progressed, but calming down did not. As he accumulated successes, his \u201cwinner or failure\u201d mentality and its resultant insecurity morphed into hypercompetitive intensity on and off the mound. New Britain dispatched the Phillies and faced the Lynn Sailors for the championship, which they won when Clemens pitched a 10-strikeout shutout in Game Four . After he had breezed through two levels of the minor leagues and won his second championship in three months, Clemens\u2019 baseball future looked bright. His personal life became...",

{
  "name": "Jesse Barfield",
  "bio": "Jesse Barfield July 21, 2021 / in BioProject - Person Home Run Champions 1980s All-Stars Parent-Child / by admin LnRiLWhlYWRpbmcuaGFzLWJhY2tncm91bmR7cGFkZGluZzowfQ==   This article was written by Malcolm Allen Arm and wrist strength were Jesse Barfield\u2019s calling cards. Over 12 major-league seasons (1981-1992) with the Toronto Blue Jays and New York Yankees, the two-time Gold Glove right-fielder\u2019s throwing arm was so powerful that he gunned down 162 baserunners. His swing produced a home run every 19.7 at-bats, including an American League-leading 40 in 1986, before a series of injuries and wrist surgeries derailed his career. Jesse Lee Barfield was born on October 29, 1959, in Joliet, Illinois, roughly 30 miles southwest of Chicago. His mother, Annie J. Barfield, had just turned 18. \u201cMy father abandoned us and decided to marry another woman. So that\u2019s when my mom met a man named Jesse who stepped in, and he and my mom started dating. I was actually named after him,\u201d Barfield explained. \u201cEveryone in my family told me that he was my dad.\u201d 1 Annie eventually married another man and had three more children: Darryl, Melanie and Eric. Annie divorced around the time Jesse became a teen and worked as a seamstress to support Jesse and his three siblings. \u201cWe made a tough situation great,\u201d Jesse said. \u201cI owe my mom a lot of credit because we didn\u2019t actually know we were poor, I don\u2019t think we were, I mean, we didn\u2019t miss any meals.\u201d 2 The George Werden Buck Boys Club was Jesse\u2019s home away from home. \u201cIt was a lifesaver for me,\u201d he said. 3 He perfected his ping pong skills enough to win some events and finish second in a city tournament. Basketball, shooting pool and swimming also kept him out of trouble. Despite pressure to root for either the Cubs or White Sox, he recalled, \u201cI had heroes on both teams.\u201d 4 White Sox\u2019 slugger Dick Allen was one, but Ernie Banks was his favorite. He watched Mr. Cub play every day on WGN-TV and imitated his batting stance. Jesse was 12 when he joined his first baseball team at the repeated prodding of his friend Rick, who urged him to try out for the Belmont Little League. He started as a shortstop and pitcher. 5 Jesse\u2019s passion for the sport developed quickly. On a Boys Club trip to Comiskey Park when he was 13, Barfield recalled, \u201cWe used to sit up in the right-field upper deck area\u2026I told the guys, no joke, \u2018Man, one day I\u2019m going to playing right out there. You watch.\u2019\u201d 6 They laughed. Jesse played Pony League and Colt League ball in Ingalls Park, and made both the basketball and baseball teams at Joliet Central High School. As a junior, he ripped a game-winning double off Joliet Catholic pitcher Bill Gullickson .\u201d 7 Since Gullickson would be the number-two overall draft pick the following year, scouts from a handful of big-league teams witnessed it, including Bobby Mattick \u2013who joined the expansion Toronto Blue Jays a few months later. \u201cBobby liked the way Jesse swung the bat,\u201d recalled scout Al LaMacchia . \u201cThe ball he hit was a double that put a hole in the wooden fence.\u201d 8 After Barfield earned first team All-American honors as a senior, the Blue Jays selected him in the ninth round of the 1977 June Amateur Draft. Toronto offered $2,500, but Barfield had other options. He\u2019d won a statewide drafting contest and earned a scholarship to study architectural drawing at Bradley University in Peoria. 9 After the Blue Jays tripled their bonus offer, he signed with scout Bob Engle. 10 Jesse bought his mother a new Ford Thunderbird. 11 He debuted with Utica (New York) in the short-season, Single-A New York-Pennsylvania League in 1977. Jesse\u2019s mom drove him to the Rawlings Adirondack factory 35 miles away in Dolgeville and loaded him up with two dozen customized bats. 12 Barfield\u2019s first professional homer was a game-winning, three-run shot, but he went deep only five times in 70 games and batted .226. 13 \u201cThe first time I saw a slider in the rookie league, I couldn\u2019t hit it,\u201d he recalled. \u201cI just made a half-swing.\u201d 14 That fall in the Florida Instructional League, the Blue Jays opened up his stance to help him track the ball better.\u201d 15 Barfield\u2019s first full-length season was 1978 with Dunedin in the Single-A Florida State League, where chronic losing in front of small crowds made baseball feel more like a job. 16 In 133 games, he produced only two home runs and a .206 batting average while striking out a league-high 125 times. \u201cYou\u2019re not going to do well every day,\u201d he reflected. \u201cIf you can\u2019t accept this game the way it is, then you might as well get into something else.\u201d 17 Barfield\u2019s throwing arm was already big-league, as evidenced by his circuit-best 22 outfield assists. \u201cI believe my fielding is what kept me in the game until I learned how to hit,\u201d he said. 18 In 1979, Barfield led the Kinston (North Carolina) Eagles of the Single-A Carolina League with 37 extra-base hits and raised his average to .264 in 136 games. The Blue Jays added him to their 40-man roster. He became an offseason resident of Houston, where his mother had moved for work. 19 Toronto had a Double-A affiliate for the first time in 1980, and Barfield hit the first home run in the history of the Knoxville Blue Jays. 20 Despite missing the last three weeks with a broken thumb, he paced the Southern League team with 14 homers, 65 RBIs, and 63 runs scored in 124 contests. 21 Barfield healed in time for winter ball in Colombia and returned wearing a gold pendant featuring the Blue Jays logo. 22 \u201cThe guys give me a lot of noise over it,\u201d he admitted. \u201cThey say, \u2018What you gonna do if you get traded?\u2019\u201d 23 Billy Smith , Toronto\u2019s player development director, encouraged him, saying, \u201cYou\u2019re a can\u2019t-miss if you keep the right attitude.\u201d 24 Barfield returned to Knoxville in 1981 and earned team MVP honors, batting .261 with 16 homers in 141 games. 25 His all-around display included professional highs in triples (13), stolen bases (25), and a league-best 23 outfield assists. The Blue Jays were on the road when they called Barfield up to the majors. He was to join the team at Comiskey Park, where his Boys Club friends had laughed at him. \u201cAfter I called my mom first, you\u2019d better believe I got on the horn to talk to those guys,\u201d he said. 26 For his debut on September 3, Barfield recalled, \u201cI was so fresh that they had to staple my number to the uniform. 27 He stroked an RBI single to center against lefty Steve Trout in his second at-bat and stole second base after reaching first base on a fielder\u2019s choice in the seventh inning. In the series finale on September 6, Barfield blasted his first big-league homer off Britt Burns . He hit safely in his first eight contests and finished the season with a .232 average and two homers in 25 games, plus lessons from hitting coach and Hall-of-Famer Bobby Doerr . \u201cBobby talked to me about picking the right pitch, swinging at strikes,\u201d Barfield recalled. 28 That winter, Toronto GM Pat Gillick said he wanted Barfield to gain Triple-A experience in 1982. 29 Barfield led the Venezuelan League\u2019s Cardenales de Lara in RBIs despite coming home early after losing 18 pounds from a virus. 30 He gained the weight back and more working out with a Nautilus machine. 31 \u201cHe\u2019s put on 10 pounds, and it\u2019s all muscle,\u201d Gillick remarked in spring training. Barfield\u2019s Grapefruit League performance convinced manager Bobby Cox to make him Toronto\u2019s Opening Day right fielder. \u201cI just like everything I\u2019ve seen about him,\u201d Cox explained. 32 \u201cAll Jesse needs is experience here. If he\u2019s struggling with his bat, he\u2019s still going to help you defensively.\u201d 33 Sharing outfield time with lefty Hosken Powell , Barfield batted .268 in 46 games before hyperextending his left knee diving and sliding onto Exhibition Stadium\u2019s artificial turf to snare a line drive on June 3. After returning 10 days later, he hit .236 in 93 appearances. 34 Barfield\u2019s first homer of the season was a pinch-hit grand slam on April 24. During the final week of the season, he launched what he called \u201cthe longest hit I\u2019ve ever had in my life\u201d \u2013 a 450-foot blast off Minnesota\u2019s Jack O\u2019Connor . 35 Overall, the rookie produced 18 homers in 394 at-bats to rank second on the Blue Jays. Barfield also enjoyed a breakthrough year off the field. Though he\u2019d never been a heavy drinker or a smoker, he described his early career priorities as \u201cmyself, money, and women\u201d and said, \u201cIf you called me a no-good so-and-so, we were gonna throw down.\u201d That changed when he became a committed Christian during a Bible study at the home of pitcher Roy Lee Jackson . \u201c[Jackson] seemed to have this inner peace, whether he gave up one hit or nine runs, that I\u2019d never seen before,\u201d 36 Barfield remarked. That summer, Jesse married Marla Travis, a Houstonian. Their first child, Josh, was born that December in Venezuela, where Jesse slugged 12 homers in 44 games (including playoffs) for Lara. 37 \u201cI think eight of my 12 homers there were against right-handed pitchers,\u201d he said. 38 He platooned again in 1983 and kept working with hitting instructor Cito Gaston . \u201cCito was like a big brother to me,\u201d Barfield said. 39 \u201cHe took me from basically a pull hitter to a guy that can drive the ball to all fields.\u201d 40 Through July 21, however, Barfield was batting only .197. Jackson and centerfielder Lloyd Moseby encouraged him. \u201cWe kept [Barfield] up spiritually,\u201d Moseby explained. \u201cWe didn\u2019t even talk baseball because we knew he had all the ability.\u201d 41 On July 26, Barfield went deep in each game of a doubleheader against the White Sox, including his first career opposite-field homer. \u201cThat showed me something,\u201d he said. \u201cI can drive the ball the other way and be successful.\u201d 42 Beginning that night, Barfield batted .317 with 16 home runs in his final 56 games. Between August 29 and September 2, he went deep seven times in five days. He explained that he\u2019d backed away from home plate slightly to give himself an extra split-second to extend his arms and drive the ball. 43 By connecting for 27 homers in 388 at-bats during the Blue Jays\u2019 first-ever winning season, he earned a two-year contract. He\u2019d produced both a better slugging percentage and OPS against righthanders, so he wanted to play every day in 1984. \u201cYou\u2019ve got to find a spot for me,\u201d Barfield said. \u201cI want to be an All-Star.\u201d 44 After a poor April, however, the 6-foot-1, 200-pounder wound up playing less frequently, starting fewer than half the games. Midway through a season in which Barfield batted .284 with 14 homers in 320 at-bats, Angels slugger Reggie Jackson said, \u201cI\u2019d compare Jesse Barfield to me, a guy who hits the ball a mile and strikes out a lot. The thing about Jesse, he doesn\u2019t play enough. You\u2019ve got to be up at that plate, learning.\u201d 45 After the Blue Jays dealt outfielder Dave Collins and shortstop Alfredo Griffin to Oakland for All-Star closer Bill Caudill that offseason, they told Barfield that right field was his to lose. \u201cI didn\u2019t like the sound of that, so I\u2019ve got to go out and win it,\u201d he said. 46 On April 17, his sudden death homer against the Rangers lifted Toronto back to .500. The team moved into first place to stay during Barfield\u2019s personal-best 16-game hitting streak in May. He finished the season batting .289 in 155 games, with 27 homers and a career-high 22 steals to become the first 20-20 player in franchise history. 47 The Blue Jays made the playoffs for the first time, and the local writers voted him the club\u2019s player of the year. 48 Barfield went deep once and batted .280 in the ALCS against the Royals, but Toronto fell in seven games. It was to be his only postseason experience. Though they\u2019d started together the night of Barfield\u2019s big-league debut, 1985 was the first year that George Bell , Lloyd Moseby, and Barfield were Toronto\u2019s regular outfield trio. They soon became the AL\u2019s top such unit. Bell, a hard-hitting Dominican, played left. Moseby, a speedy lefty, manned center. Barfield had the most power and defensive ability. \u201cYou can never play any better right field. It\u2019s impossible,\u201d insisted Cox. 49 \u201cThere is little in baseball, there is little in any sport, to match the splendid thrill of watching Jesse Barfield make a play in right field and make one of his glorious throws to catch the runner at the plate,\u201d wrote John Slinger in the Toronto Star . 50 Barfield led all major-league outfielders with 22 assists and eight double plays in 1985. Barfield and the Blue Jays started slowly under new manager Jimy Williams in 1986. \u201cThe pitchers try to find different ways to get you out,\u201d Barfield observed. \u201cThey put you under a microscope looking for a weakness they can exploit.\u201d 51 Though pitching struggles doomed Toronto to a fourth-place finish, Barfield adjusted and belted 40 home runs to lead the majors. \u201cI\u2019m just trying to drive the ball,\u201d he said. \u201cAnd when I do that, the homers usually come on their own.\u201d 52 He played in his only All-Star Game that year at the Houston Astrodome , won the first of two straight Gold Gloves and edged Montreal Expos\u2019 star Tim Raines to become the first Blue Jay ever honored as Canada\u2019s Baseball Man of the Year. 53 That summer, Barfield received a phone call from a woman who identified herself as his grandmother. She revealed that a neighborhood man he\u2019d known growing up named Evell Kelly was his biological father. Jesse\u2019s mother confirmed it. \u201cI guess for many reasons she never wanted me to know,\u201d Barfield said. He got to know Evell, and they shared a resemblance as well as outgoing personalities, but Jesse was already 26 when he learned the truth. \u201cA father-son relationship is something I was deprived of,\u201d he said. 54 Barfield\u2019s $1,525,000 salary for 1987 made him the highest paid non-pitcher in Blue Jays history at the time. 55 By June 27, Toronto boasted the majors\u2019 best record and he had gone deep 19 times. After combining with Bell to bash 71 homers the previous year, they\u2019d hit 75 in 1987. \u201c[Bell] hits the crowd pleasers, high and you have time to stand and cheer,\u201d Barfield said. \u201cMine just jump. Usually line drives.\u201d 56 Barfield hit two-homers against the Rangers on consecutive Friday nights in early May, including a sudden-death shot off Mitch Williams . In June, he beat Baltimore with another walk-off. \u201cThe biggest thing about Jesse Barfield, as far as baseball, is...",

  "bio": "Jay Buhner April 7, 2021 / in BioProject - Person 1990s All-Stars 2001 Seattle Mariners / by admin LnRiLWhlYWRpbmcuaGFzLWJhY2tncm91bmR7cGFkZGluZzowfQ==   This article was written by Douglas Jordan From 1995 through 1997, Jay Buhner was as prolific a home run hitter as any player in baseball. During those three years his 124 home runs exceeded those of his celebrated teammate Ken Griffey Jr. (122), 1995 MVP Mo Vaughn (118), 1996 MVP Juan Gonzalez (116), and 1993-94 MVP Frank Thomas (115). In addition to his prodigious power, Buhner was also a leader and vital on-field contributor to the 1995 Seattle Mariner team that earned the franchise\u2019s first playoff berth since it was established in 1977. Buhner\u2019s role with that historic team, along with his outgoing personality and never-ending hustle on the field, made him a fan favorite in Seattle. Jay Campbell Buhner was born August 13, 1964, in Louisville, Kentucky. His parents, David Carl Buhner and Kay Cantrell Rose Buhner, were both graduates of the University of Kentucky who married in 1961. 1 David passed his love of hunting, fishing, and baseball on to his three sons: Jay, Ted, and Shawn. 2 David and Kay, and Jay\u2019s maternal grandfather, C.C. Rose, were instrumental in Jay\u2019s development as a baseball player. \u201cWithout my parents and my grandparents,\u201d Buhner said, \u201cno way I would have made it to the major leagues. No way. They were the batting practice pitchers, the shaggers, the car pool leaders, the number one fans. They did everything.\u201d 3 Jay wasn\u2019t the only professional baseball player in the family. His brother Shawn had a six-year career in the Mariner minor league system, peaking with one season in AAA. Jay spent his first 14 years in Louisville before the family moved to Philadelphia and then to Texas, where his father worked as a chemist at Dixie Chemical in Houston. 4 He played high school ball at Clear Creek High School near League City, Texas, under coach Jim Mallory. Mallory gave Buhner his nickname, \u201cBone.\u201d After Buhner took a ball off his head but still made the play, Mallory commented that it was a good thing Jay had a bony head. 5 \u201cBonehead\u201d was shortened to \u201cBone\u201d and the nickname stuck. Buhner did not always dream of playing in the big leagues. \u201cWhen I was younger, I never thought there was life after high school baseball. I never thought about the pros or even college. I didn\u2019t know there was such a thing as a scholarship.\u201d He went to McLennan Community College in Waco, Texas, because two of his Clear Creek teammates were going there. McLennan turned out to be a great fit for the sometimes-mercurial Buhner. Head coach Rick Butler was a strict disciplinarian who had a positive influence on Jay on and off the field. Buhner credits Butler with turning him into an excellent defensive outfielder. \u201cIf we misplayed a ball in practice, we had to do what we called a supersprint,\u201d Buhner recalled. \u201cYou had to drop your glove and run to the opposite foul line and back in less than 60 seconds. That\u2019s where I first took pride in my defense.\u201d Buhner\u2019s contributions on both defense and offense (he batted .327 and led the team in home runs) helped McLennan win the junior college national title in 1983. 6 His play at McLennan drew the attention of big-league scouts. Drafted by the Atlanta Braves in the ninth round of the 1983 draft, he elected not to sign. Drafted by the Pittsburgh Pirates during the second round of the secondary free agent draft in January 1984, he signed on May 26, 1984. Buhner remained in the Yankee minor league system for three-and-one-half seasons before being called up to the big club for seven games in September 1987. After starting the 1988 season in AAA, he returned to the Yankees in May and began playing regularly in the outfield in June. Buhner found his time with the Yankees very rewarding. \u201cThe great thing about the Yankees was that every level I played at, we won. Bucky Dent was my manager at every level, and he was awesome. He taught me how to win. Then when I moved up to the big leagues, I was surrounded by veterans who were great to me: Dave Winfield , Jack Clark , Jose Cruz , Rickey Henderson , Don Mattingly . I couldn\u2019t have asked for a better place to break in.\u201d 8 But the rookie with potential was traded to the Seattle Mariners at the trading deadline for a veteran with power, Ken Phelps . The trade has been questioned ever since, and became famous nationally when on the Seinfeld television show, Jerry Stiller (as Frank Costanza) berates Larry David (as George Steinbrenner ) with the line, \u201cWhat did you trade Jay Buhner for? \u2026 You don\u2019t know what you\u2019re doing!\u201d 9 Upon joining the Mariners, Buhner was immediately inserted into the lineup, but he struggled at the plate. Although he hit ten home runs in 60 games that year, he batted just .224 and struck out 68 times in 192 at bats. Jay started the 1989 season in AAA Calgary and was asked to work on cutting down his strikeout rate. 10 He was called back up to the big club on June 2, but injured his right wrist on June 27 when he slammed into the outfield wall against Kansas City. 11 The time in Calgary proved helpful, however. In 58 games he had a .275 batting average, just 55 strikeouts in 204 at bats, and hit nine home runs. Buhner did not start the 1990 season with the Mariners \u2014 but for a different reason. This time he suffered a sprained right ankle on a pick-off play during spring training. 12 He was anxious when he returned to the Mariners on June 1. \u201cI\u2019m glad this one is over with. I was so nervous I think I went to the restroom ten times.\u201d The nerves didn\u2019t impact his performance. Buhner hit a grand slam in his first at bat after returning from the injured list. But the injury bug bit him again soon thereafter. He broke a bone in his right forearm when he was hit by a pitch in the June 16 game against the Rangers and was out of action until August 23. 13 For the 1990 season, Buhner played in 51 games, hit seven balls out of the park, and had a .276 batting average with 50 strikeouts in 163 at bats. After four seasons splitting time between the majors and minors, Buhner\u2019s apprenticeship was over. Beginning with the 1991 season, he became the Mariners starting right fielder. On May 30, a first inning, three-run home run off Kenny Rogers of the Texas Rangers set the stage for a six-RBI game. A couple of months later, he put on an impressive display of power in consecutive series against the Orioles and Yankees. On July 20 in Baltimore, he smashed a home run that travelled 463 feet. Five days later he bettered that mark with a 479-foot blast at Yankee Stadium. Of the home run at Yankee Stadium, Buhner said, \u201cEvery player has his career shot and I think that is mine.\u201d 14 A few weeks later on August 12, Buhner hit two home runs off reigning Cy Young Award-winning pitcher Bob Welch . Those two homers, and another long ball two days later, gave him a total of seven dingers against his former Yankee teammates since being traded to the Mariners. 16 Maybe Frank Costanza was right! Buhner continued to improve in 1993. He had three games that season with four RBIs. In one of those games, on May 17, he hit a three-run home run and a solo homer against the Rangers in Texas. He continued to pound out home runs at Yankee Stadium with two more over the course of the season. He had 17 home runs by the All-Star break, and 27 on the season. He finished the season with new highs in games played (158), hits (153), doubles (28), and RBIs (98). His .272 batting average was just a few points below his career high of .276. As befitted the pride he took in his arm and glove play, Buhner wanted to draw attention to the trio\u2019s defensive prowess. He said, \u201cI wish there was more talk about our defense. All you hear about is how many home runs we\u2019re capable of hitting. I think we\u2019re all capable of winning a Gold Glove.\u201d 17 Buhner\u2019s comment proved prescient. Although Anthony never won one, both Griffey Jr. and Buhner won Gold Gloves in 1996. It wasn\u2019t just talk from Buhner, either. On July 1, 1994, with the Mariners leading, 4-3, in the bottom of the ninth, Buhner slammed into the right field wall at Yankee Stadium taking a potential game-tying hit away from Randy Velarde . 18 Griffey called it, \u201cThe best catch I\u2019ve seen Jay make.\u201d Buhner bruised his pelvis on the play and couldn\u2019t take right field again until after the All-Star break. In addition to his stellar defense, Buhner\u2019s offense also could not be ignored in 1994. On April 19, he hit two more home runs at Yankee Stadium on the way to another four-RBI game. (He finished his career with 19 home runs in the Bronx ballpark.) His season total of 21 home runs in 1994 was lower than the previous three seasons, but the players went on strike on August 12 (ending the season) so Buhner played in just 101 games that year. At the rate he was hitting long balls, he would have had a career-high 31 homers if he had played in 150 games. He was also on pace to drive in 100 runs, and finished the shortened season with a career high .279 batting average. The Mariners took advantage of Jay\u2019s popularity with Seattle baseball fans. Buhner\u2019s signature bald head inspired the marketing promotion Buhner Buzz Cut Night from 1994 to 2001. Fans received free admission if they were bald or had their head shaved before the game. Buhner himself would sometimes help shave heads. The number of participants was an indication of the growth of Buhner\u2019s popularity. In 1994, the first year of the promotion, 512 fans (including two women) participated. During the last year of the event, 6246 fans (including 112 women) took part. 19 Playing on his nickname, Buhner\u2019s walk-up song before his at bats was \u201cBad to the Bone\u201d by George Thorogood and the Destroyers. And the section of seats behind him in right field was referred to as \u201cThe Boneyard.\u201d Before the 1995 season, Buhner was offered a lucrative four-year $18 million contract by the Orioles. He declined that offer and accepted a three-year $15.5 contract with the Mariners, saying, \u201cWe have a chance to win the division next season and hopefully a new stadium. I want to be around to see that.\u201d 20 Strong lobbying by Ken Griffey Jr. on Buhner\u2019s behalf helped persuade Mariner management to increase their offer to Buhner. A grateful Buhner offered to pay for all of Griffey\u2019s lunches during the season. Griffey joked, \u201cI will make a list of all the places I want to eat and give them to Jay before each road trip.\u201d 21 By 1995, the Mariners and Buhner were both ready to live up to their potential. Buhner entered the season as a 30-year old with eight seasons of big league experience. He was poised for a big season, and he had it. He played in 126 games, drove in 121 runs, and crushed a then career-high 40 home runs, tying Frank Thomas for second place in the American League behind Albert Belle \u2019s 50. Buhner had two games with five RBIs, and six other games where he drove in four runs. In two of his four-RBI games, the four runs came in with a lone swing of the bat as Jay cleared the bases with a grand slam. Buhner placed a career-high fifth in the MVP voting, behind winner Mo Vaughn. It\u2019s difficult to separate Buhner\u2019s personal accomplishments in 1995 from the Mariners\u2019 success that year. The Mariners were just five games out of first place at the end of June. But sub-.500 play in July left them trailing the Angels by 13 games on August 2 before they started to climb back into contention. By the end of August, they were back in the race, trailing the Angels by only seven and one-half games. For baseball fans in Seattle, the pennant race of September 1995 was unforgettable. Buhner was a key contributor to the Mariners\u2019 campaign during the crucial stretch run. His leadership was also instrumental. Jay said, \u201cThe session ended with the players committed to putting our guts on the field every day and see what happens.\u201d 22 The team responded with seven wins in the next ten games. In three games September 11-13, he drove in 13 runs. For the month, he hit 14 home runs and had 33 RBIs. The Mariners slowly closed on the Angels and climbed into a tie for first place on September 20. The two teams stayed close the rest of the month, and finished the season in a dead heat. The Mariners won a coin toss to determine home field, and then won the single game at the Kingdome to decide the division crown behind a pitching gem by Randy Johnson . The Mariners beat the Yankees in the divisional round before falling to the Indians in the ALCS.
